did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper developed an explainable multimodal AI framework for skin lesion risk prediction. It utilized Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) with transfer learning on 3D total body photography (TBP) images, machine learning models including XGBoost, CatBoost, and logistic regression on clinical data, and multimodal fusion techniques. Interpretability was assessed using SHAP and Class Activation Maps (CAM).

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work developing an explainable multimodal AI framework for skin lesion risk prediction. It describes specific methods including data acquisition from the ISIC 2024 dataset (1,075 patients), implementation of CNNs and machine learning models (XGBoost, CatBoost, logistic regression), and reports original results such as accuracy, recall, F1 scores, and AUC values for various lesion types. The structure includes standard research sections: Abstract, Introduction, Methods, Results, and Conclusion.

title: Explainable multimodal AI for skin lesion risk prediction via 3D imaging and clinical data
brief_summary: This study developed an explainable multimodal AI framework to classify six common skin lesion types by integrating 3D total body photography (TBP) images with structured clinical data from the ISIC 2024 dataset of 1,075 patients. The methodology employed Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) with transfer learning on 3D TBP images, machine learning models like XGBoost on clinical features, and a multimodal fusion strategy to enhance diagnostic accuracy. Model interpretability was assessed using Shapley Additive Explanations (SHAP) and Class Activation Maps (CAM) to visualize key predictors such as lesion asymmetry and color heterogeneity. The multimodal fusion model substantially outperformed unimodal approaches, achieving recall and F1 scores above 95% and Area Under the Curve (AUC) values exceeding 0.95 for all classes. Specifically, the model reached an AUC of 0.98 for nevus and actinic keratosis, ranking among the top-performing entries in the ISIC 2024 challenge with a partial false-positive rate of 0.1734. These results demonstrate that integrating 3D imaging with clinical data provides a transparent and clinically actionable tool for precision dermatology and early skin cancer detection.

all_task_types.target: Six-class skin lesion diagnosis (invasive melanoma, basal cell carcinoma, squamous cell carcinoma, nevus, benign not otherwise specified, and actinic keratosis)
all_task_types.details: The paper explicitly states the goal is to 'classify and assess the risk of six common skin-lesion types' using a multinomial logistic-regression model and CNNs for 'six-class skin lesion classification'. The multimodal fusion model outputs 'six-class probability vectors' to predict discrete diagnostic categories. Performance was evaluated using accuracy, recall, F1 scores, and AUC for these classes.

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2024 Dataset
datasets_used.description: An international dataset comprising records from patients who underwent 3D total body photography (TBP) between 2015 and 2024. It includes cases collected from institutions across six continents, such as Memorial Sloan Kettering Cancer Center (USA), Barcelona Clinic Hospital (Spain), Queensland University (Australia), Vienna Medical University (Austria), and Basel University Hospital (Switzerland). The dataset focuses on atypical or clinically significant lesions that have been biopsied or monitored short-term using digital dermoscopy.

use.training: Used for training machine learning models (XGBoost, CatBoost, logistic regression) on clinical features and for five-fold cross-validation of the multimodal fusion model.
use.validation: Used for validation via five-fold cross-validation for the multimodal fusion model.
use.testing: Used for testing and benchmarking against the ISIC 2024 challenge binary classification task.
use.other: Used for data augmentation (random rotation, horizontal flipping, color variation) to address class imbalance.

datasets_used.name: HAM10000 Dataset
datasets_used.description: A dermoscopic dataset used for pre-training the Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) via transfer learning before fine-tuning on the 3D TBP images.

hypothesis_testing.test: Statistical significance evaluation via p-values (specific test name not reported, likely ANOVA or Kruskal-Wallis for continuous variables and Chi-square or t-test for categorical variables based on context of comparing clinical/demographic characteristics across groups)
hypothesis_testing.null_hypothesis: There are no significant differences in clinical and demographic characteristics (age, sex, lesion size, TBP intensity/color indices, geometry, symmetry, spatial coordinates, area, perimeter) across the six skin lesion subtypes.
hypothesis_testing.alternative_hypothesis: There are significant differences in at least one of the clinical and demographic characteristics across the six skin lesion subtypes.
hypothesis_testing.interpretation: P-values were used to evaluate statistical significance of baseline comparisons. No significant differences were found for age (P=0.3907), sex (P=0.4272), lesion size (P=0.3371), TBP intensity/color indices, geometry, symmetry, spatial coordinates, area, or perimeter (all P≥0.18). The only feature demonstrating significant separation was tbp_lv_nevi_confidence (P=0.0480).

ISIC 2024 Dataset (Clinical-Only Models).metric: Accuracy, Precision, Recall, F1 Score, Precision and Recall (PR)
ISIC 2024 Dataset (Clinical-Only Models).notes: XGBoost was selected as the most accurate clinical-only model. Specific lesion-level accuracies for XGBoost: Basal cell carcinoma (BCC) 78.6%, Nevus 72.6%, Benign NOS 60.0%, Invasive melanoma 43.8%, Actinic keratosis 12.5%, Squamous cell carcinoma (SCC) 16.7%. Misclassification rates included 10.7% of BCC cases misclassified as nevi, 22.1% of nevi misclassified as BCC, 25.0% of Benign NOS misclassified as BCC, and 75.0% of SCC misdiagnosed as BCC.

ISIC 2024 Dataset (3D TBP-Only CNN Model.notes: Actinic/solar keratosis had 15.62% mislabelled as SCC. Domain adaptation alone yielded variable performance.

ISIC 2024 Dataset (Multimodal Fusion Mod.notes: AUC values exceeded 0.95 across all classes. Specific AUC of 0.98 reported for nevus and actinic keratosis. Recall and F1 scores above 95% reported for the multimodal model.

architecture_used.main_model: Multimodal fusion framework combining a Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) for 3D TBP images and an XGBoost classifier for clinical data; also evaluated unimodal models including Logistic Regression, Decision Tree, Random Forest, Gradient Boosting, SVM, CatBoost, and a multinomial logistic regression model.
architecture_used.pretraining: CNNs were pre-trained on the HAM10000 dermoscopic dataset via transfer learning before fine-tuning on 3D TBP images.

architecture_details.cnn_structure: Three convolutional blocks with convolutional layers, activation functions, and pooling operations. Two additional Conv2D layers were appended during fine-tuning for enhanced feature extraction.
architecture_details.fusion_strategy: Late fusion: CNN-generated six-class probability vectors (f_image) were concatenated with standardized clinical feature vectors (f_clinical) and processed by an XGBoost classifier.
architecture_details.clinical_features: 41 clinical and lesion-specific features extracted, including age_approx, lesion size, and TBP-derived parameters (e.g., tbp_lv_A, tbp_lv_Bext, tbp_lv_eccentricity, tbp_lv_symm_2axis).
architecture_details.multimodal_fusion_model: XGBoost classifier taking concatenated image and clinical features as input.

training_details.hardware: NVIDIA RTX 4070 GPU (8 GB RAM) with CUDA acceleration
training_details.data_augmentation: Random rotation, horizontal flipping, and color variation applied to all lesion types except nevus.

classification_or_output_head.baseline_output: Multinomial logistic regression model estimating probabilities of six distinct skin disease categories using cumulative logistic function.

preprocessing_techniques: Data augmentation: random rotation, horizontal flipping, and color variation applied to all lesion types except nevus to address class imbalance.
Image resizing: 3D TBP images resized to 128 × 128 × 3 pixels to match model input specifications.
Tensor conversion: Dataset converted to tensor form using torchvision.transforms.ToTensor().
Feature standardization: Clinical feature vectors standardized to ensure scale comparability and model stability before concatenation with image features.
Multicollinearity assessment and exclusion: Variance Inflation Factor (VIF) used to evaluate predictors; features with VIF > 10 excluded to prevent instability.
Color and contrast calibration: Rigorous calibration within the imaging system to resolve pigmentation variations and enable detection of multichromatic patterns.

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Applied to all lesion types except nevus to address class imbalance and increase data diversity without altering diagnostic labels.

conclusions: The proposed explainable multimodal AI framework, which integrates 3D total body photography (TBP) images with structured clinical data, substantially outperforms unimodal approaches for skin lesion risk prediction.

The multimodal fusion model achieved high diagnostic performance, with recall and F1 scores above 95% and Area Under the Curve (AUC) values exceeding 0.95 across all six lesion classes, including an AUC of 0.98 for nevus and actinic keratosis.
The multimodal model ranked among the top-performing entries in the ISIC 2024 challenge, achieving a partial false-positive rate of 0.1734.

Clinical-only models (e.g., XGBoost) showed moderate accuracy but suffered from significant misclassification rates, particularly for high-risk tumors like squamous cell carcinoma (75.0% misdiagnosed as basal cell carcinoma) and invasive melanoma (43.8% accuracy).
3D TBP-only CNN models demonstrated variable performance depending on the lesion type, highlighting the necessity of multimodal data fusion to address morphological and textural overlaps.

Interpretability analysis using SHAP and Class Activation Maps (CAM) identified key predictors such as lesion asymmetry (tbp_lv_symm_2axis) and visual classifier features, enhancing clinical trust and transparency.
The integration of 3D imaging with clinical data provides a transparent and clinically actionable tool for precision dermatology and early skin cancer detection.
important_remarks: The study is based on the ISIC 2024 dataset, which is an 'accepted article manuscript' (unpublished/unedited version at time of extraction) and may contain errors affecting content validity.
The dataset exhibits significant class imbalance, with Actinic Keratosis (n=39) and Squamous Cell Carcinoma (n=73) being underrepresented compared to Nevus (n=443) and Benign NOS (n=200).
Clinical-only models showed poor sensitivity for high-risk tumors: Invasive Melanoma accuracy was only 43.8%, and 75.0% of SCC lesions were misdiagnosed as Basal Cell Carcinoma.

The multimodal fusion model achieved exceptionally high performance (AUC > 0.95, Recall/F1 > 95%) via five-fold cross-validation, which may indicate optimistic bias or data leakage if the validation split was not strictly patient-level independent.

The CNN component utilized transfer learning from the HAM10000 dermoscopic dataset, introducing a domain shift between 2D dermoscopy and 3D total body photography (TBP) that was addressed only via fine-tuning.
Data augmentation (rotation, flipping, color variation) was applied to all lesion types except Nevus, potentially creating an artificial performance disparity or bias against the most frequent class.
The study lacks external validation on independent datasets or different imaging centers, limiting generalizability beyond the specific institutions contributing to the ISIC 2024 dataset.

The partial false-positive rate (pFPR) of 0.1734 was reported at a fixed sensitivity ≤ 0.20, which is a non-standard metric for clinical screening where high sensitivity is typically required; this may overstate clinical utility for early detection.
The manuscript is licensed under CC BY-NC-ND 4.0, restricting commercial use and adaptation, which limits its immediate utility for commercial AI deployment.
